name: profile-before-optimizing
description: Tests whether the model insists on profiling before applying optimizations, rather than jumping straight to code changes
trap: The 800ms latency is almost certainly from fetchFromDB (external bottleneck), not from strings.ToUpper or JSON encoding. Without the skill, the model will micro-optimize the Go code (use strings.Builder, preallocate, etc.) instead of pointing out that profiling is needed first and the bottleneck is likely external.

assertions.text: Recommends profiling (pprof, fgprof, or tracing) before making code changes
Identifies fetchFromDB as the likely bottleneck (external I/O, not Go code)
Mentions that intuition about bottlenecks is often wrong (~80% of the time)
Does NOT primarily focus on micro-optimizing strings.ToUpper or JSON encoding
Suggests investigating the database query (query tuning, caching, connection pool)

name: fgprof-off-cpu-bottleneck
description: Tests whether the model recommends fgprof for off-CPU bottlenecks instead of only standard CPU profiling
prompt: Our Go service has high latency (p99 = 2s) but CPU usage is only 5%. Standard pprof CPU profile shows almost nothing — the hot functions consume negligible CPU time. What profiling approach should we use to find the bottleneck?
trap: Standard CPU profiling only captures on-CPU time. When CPU usage is low but latency is high, the bottleneck is off-CPU (I/O wait, network, blocked goroutines). The skill specifically recommends fgprof for this. Without the skill, the model may suggest heap profiles, goroutine dumps, or other approaches that miss the key tool.

assertions.text: Recommends fgprof as the primary tool for capturing off-CPU wait time
Explains that standard pprof CPU profile only captures on-CPU time, which is why it shows nothing
Suggests the bottleneck is likely I/O wait (network, database, filesystem)
Mentions goroutine profile as a complementary diagnostic (blocked goroutines in net.Read or database/sql)
Suggests distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry) for identifying slow upstream services

name: iterative-benchmark-methodology
description: Tests whether the model follows the iterative benchmark methodology (one change at a time, benchstat comparison)
trap: Without the skill, the model will apply all optimizations at once. The skill teaches an iterative approach: write benchmark first, measure baseline with -count=6, apply ONE change at a time, compare with benchstat, then repeat.

assertions.text: Recommends writing an atomic benchmark for ProcessRecords first
Recommends measuring a baseline with -benchmem and -count=6 (or similar count for statistical significance)
Recommends applying ONE optimization at a time, not all at once
Recommends using benchstat to compare before/after with statistical significance
Suggests keeping report files as an audit trail (e.g., /tmp/report-1.txt, /tmp/report-2.txt)

name: slice-reuse-append-zero
description: Tests knowledge of the append(s[:0], ...) pattern for reusing slice backing arrays
trap: The natural approach is to declare mode outside the loop or use sync.Pool. The skill teaches the specific pattern append(mode[:0], item) to reuse the backing array with zero allocations. This is a non-obvious Go idiom.

assertions.text: Suggests using append(mode[:0], item) to reuse the backing array
Explains that reslicing to zero length retains the backing array, avoiding allocation
Moves the mode variable declaration outside the loop to enable reuse
Does NOT suggest sync.Pool as the primary solution for this simple case

name: direct-indexing-vs-append
description: Tests whether the model prefers direct indexing over append when output size equals input size
trap: The code already preallocates capacity. Without the skill, the model may not realize that make([]T, len) with direct assignment is faster than make([]T, 0, cap) with append, because direct assignment avoids per-element bounds checking and length increment.

assertions.text: Suggests using make([]Result, len(input)) with direct assignment result[i] = convert(...)
Explains that direct assignment avoids per-element append overhead (bounds check, length increment)
Notes that append is better when the result might be smaller (filtering)

name: map-range-double-lookup
description: Tests whether the model catches redundant map lookups in range loops
trap: The pattern 'for k := range m { use(m[k]) }' does two lookups per iteration. The fix is 'for k, v := range m { use(v) }'. This is a subtle optimization that many developers miss.

assertions.text: Identifies that for k := range in { in[k] } does two map lookups per iteration
Suggests for k, v := range in { result[k] = strconv.Itoa(v) } to do a single lookup

name: sentinel-errors-hot-path
description: Tests whether the model recommends preallocated sentinel errors over fmt.Errorf in hot paths
trap: The second error ("value exceeds maximum") has no dynamic content and can be a preallocated sentinel (errors.New at package level), zero allocations. The first error has dynamic content (%d) so fmt.Errorf is appropriate there. Without the skill, the model may try to optimize both the same way or miss the distinction.

assertions.text: Converts the static error (exceeds maximum) to a preallocated sentinel using errors.New at package level
Keeps fmt.Errorf for the dynamic error that includes %d (or uses a similar dynamic approach)
Explains that fmt.Errorf allocates on every call, while sentinels allocate once
Does NOT convert the dynamic error to a sentinel (it needs the value)

name: interface-boxing-hot-path
description: Tests knowledge of interface boxing allocation cost and the fix using generics or typed parameters
trap: Passing concrete types through any/interface{} forces heap allocation for boxing. The skill teaches using typed parameters or generics. Without it, the model may focus on the type switch optimization rather than the fundamental boxing problem.

assertions.text: Identifies interface boxing (any parameter) as the source of allocations
Suggests typed functions (e.g., SumInts([]int)) or generics (func Sum[T ~int|~float64]([]T)) to eliminate boxing
Explains that each concrete value passed through any requires a heap allocation for boxing
Does NOT focus only on the type switch as the optimization target

name: backing-array-leak-slice
description: Tests whether the model catches backing array retention when returning a small reslice of a large slice
trap: The reslice message[:32] retains the entire backing array (1-10MB) because it shares the same underlying array. The fix is to copy the 32 bytes to a new slice. Without the skill, the model may not identify this as a memory leak pattern.

assertions.text: Identifies that message[:32] retains the entire backing array (1-10MB)
Suggests using copy() to create an independent 32-byte slice
Explains that the original large array cannot be GC'd while the reslice exists
Provides correct copy-based fix: make([]byte, 32) + copy(header, message[:32])

name: substring-memory-leak
description: Tests knowledge of the strings.Clone pattern for substring memory leaks
trap: Substrings share the backing array of the original string. Each 36-char id retains the full 500-2000 byte log line. The skill teaches strings.Clone (Go 1.20+) as the fix. Without it, the model may not know about strings.Clone or may suggest string([]byte(s)) which is also correct but less idiomatic.

assertions.text: Identifies that substrings share the backing array of the original string
Suggests strings.Clone(line[12:48]) to create an independent copy
Explains that each 36-char ID retains the entire 500-2000 byte log line in memory

name: map-never-shrinks
description: Tests knowledge that Go maps never release bucket memory and the compact/recreate pattern
trap: Go maps grow but never shrink their bucket allocation. Deleting entries doesn't free the underlying memory. The skill teaches the compact/recreate pattern (create a new map and copy entries). Without it, the model may suggest GC tuning or other approaches that don't address the root cause.

assertions.text: Explains that Go maps never release bucket memory when entries are deleted
Suggests periodically recreating the map by copying entries to a new map
Provides the compact pattern: make(map[K]V, len(old)) + range copy
Does NOT suggest that calling runtime.GC() or tuning GOGC will fix this

name: sync-pool-rules
description: Tests proper sync.Pool usage: reset before Put, return copies not pooled buffers, size limits
trap: Multiple issues: (1) HandleRequest returns the pooled buffer directly via w.Write — the caller (net/http) may retain it after Put, (2) HandleLargeUpload puts enormous buffers (100MB+) back into the pool — don't pool objects >32KB, (3) the pool stores []byte values not pointers, which causes an allocation on Get. The skill covers all these rules.

assertions.text: Identifies that HandleLargeUpload puts oversized buffers (100MB+) back into the pool
Mentions the 32KB guideline — don't pool objects larger than ~32KB
Identifies that w.Write(buf) may retain the buffer after bufPool.Put(buf) in the same function
Suggests pooling pointers (*[]byte) instead of values to avoid allocation on Get
Recommends resetting/clearing state before Put to avoid retaining large object graphs

name: struct-field-alignment
description: Tests knowledge of struct field ordering for optimal memory layout
prompt:
Our Go service creates millions of these structs. Memory profiling shows they consume more space than expected. Can we reduce memory usage?

```go
type Event struct {
    Active    bool
    Timestamp int64
    Priority  bool
    UserID    int32
    Processed bool
    Score     float64
}
```

How large is this struct and can we make it smaller?
trap: The struct has poor field alignment. bool (1 byte) followed by int64 (8 bytes) adds 7 bytes of padding. The skill teaches reordering fields largest-to-smallest and using fieldalignment tool. Without it, the model may not compute the correct size or suggest the optimal reordering.

assertions.text: Identifies that the struct has wasted padding bytes due to alignment
Suggests reordering fields from largest to smallest (int64/float64 first, then int32, then bools)
Provides a reordered struct that is smaller than the original
Mentions the fieldalignment tool for automated detection
States alignment requirements (bool=1, int32=4, int64/float64=8)

name: zero-size-field-end-of-struct
description: Tests knowledge that struct{} at end of struct adds word-sized padding
trap: When the last field has zero size (struct{}), the compiler adds word-sized padding (8 bytes on 64-bit) to prevent a pointer to that field from overlapping the next memory block. The fix is to move struct{} to the beginning. This is a very obscure Go internals detail.

assertions.text: Explains that a zero-size field at the end of a struct causes word-sized padding
Explains the reason: preventing a pointer to the zero-size field from overlapping the next memory block
Suggests moving struct{} to the beginning of the struct to eliminate the padding
Shows the fix: type Entry struct { Flag struct{}; Value int64 } which is 8 bytes

name: map-pointer-vs-value-tradeoff
description: Tests knowledge of map[K]*V vs map[K]V tradeoff for large frequently-updated structs
prompt:
Our Go game server updates player scores frequently. This pattern is inefficient:

```go
type Player struct {
    Name      string
    Score     int
    Level     int
    Inventory [256]byte
    Stats     [64]float64
}

var players = make(map[string]Player)

func UpdateScore(id string, delta int) {
    p := players[id]
    p.Score += delta
    players[id] = p // full copy
}
```

How can we optimize the update pattern?
trap: Map values are not addressable — you can't do players[id].Score += delta. The copy-modify-reassign pattern copies the entire large struct. Using map[string]*Player allows direct modification. But the skill also teaches the tradeoff: pointer maps add GC pressure from separate heap allocations. Without it, the model may suggest pointers without mentioning the tradeoff.

assertions.text: Suggests using map[string]*Player to allow direct field modification
Explains that map values are not addressable (can't modify in place)
Shows players[id].Score += delta with pointer map
Mentions the tradeoff: pointer maps add GC pressure from separate heap allocations
Notes that for small, mostly-read structs, map[K]V (value) is better

name: inlining-log-in-hot-path
description: Tests knowledge that log calls prevent function inlining
trap: The log.Printf calls prevent the compiler from inlining the function. In a tight loop called millions of times, function call overhead is significant. The skill specifically warns about logging in hot loops preventing inlining. Without it, the model may focus on the log formatting cost rather than the inlining prevention.

assertions.text: Identifies that log calls prevent the function from being inlined by the compiler
Suggests removing log calls from the hot-path function or moving them outside
Mentions using go build -gcflags="-m" to verify inlining decisions
Explains that function call overhead matters when called millions of times in a tight loop

name: value-receiver-inlining
description: Tests knowledge that value receivers enable inlining for fluent method chains
trap: Pointer receivers add indirection that blocks inlining of fluent method chains. Value receivers allow the compiler to fully inline the chain. The skill quantifies this as -80% time. Without the skill, the model may suggest unrelated optimizations.

assertions.text: Suggests changing to value receivers instead of pointer receivers
Explains that value receivers allow the compiler to inline the fluent chain
Explains that pointer receivers add indirection that blocks inlining
Shows the value receiver signature: func (c Config) WithTimeout(d time.Duration) Config

name: cache-locality-matrix-traversal
description: Tests knowledge of row-major vs column-major traversal and cache effects
trap: Column-first traversal on row-major storage causes cache misses on every access. The fix is to swap loop order. The skill quantifies the difference as 10-50x from cache effects alone. Without it, the model may suggest parallelism or SIMD rather than the simple loop reorder.

assertions.text: Identifies the column-first traversal as the cause (cache misses)
Explains that Go stores 2D arrays in row-major order
Suggests swapping loop order to row-first (outer loop over rows)
Mentions the performance difference from cache effects (10-50x or similar magnitude)
Does NOT primarily suggest parallelism or SIMD as the first fix

name: contiguous-2d-allocation
description: Tests knowledge of contiguous 2D allocation for cache-friendly matrix access
trap: Allocating each row separately scatters data across the heap. The skill teaches the contiguous allocation pattern: single make([]float64, rows*cols) backed by one array, then slicing into rows. Without it, the model may suggest minor improvements instead of the fundamentally different allocation strategy.

assertions.text: Identifies that per-row allocation scatters data across the heap
Suggests single contiguous allocation: make([]float64, rows*cols)
Shows slicing the contiguous array into row views: data[i*cols : (i+1)*cols]
Explains that contiguous memory improves cache locality for sequential access

name: soa-vs-aos
description: Tests knowledge of Struct of Arrays vs Array of Structs for single-field iteration
trap: Loading each 64-byte Particle to read only X (8 bytes) wastes 87.5% of cache space. The skill teaches SoA (Struct of Arrays) where all X values are contiguous for 100% cache utilization. Without it, the model may suggest parallelism or preallocation rather than the data layout change.

assertions.text: Identifies that loading entire Particle structs wastes cache space when only X is needed
Suggests Struct of Arrays (SoA) layout with separate slices for X, Y, Z, etc.
Explains cache utilization improvement (contiguous X values vs scattered across structs)
Notes that AoS is fine when accessing all fields together or for small structs

name: false-sharing-concurrent-counters
description: Tests knowledge of false sharing and cache-line padding
trap: All four int64 fields fit within a single 64-byte cache line. When different goroutines update different fields, each write invalidates the other core's cache line (false sharing). The fix is cache-line padding. Without the skill, the model may suggest mutexes or sharding rather than identifying false sharing.

assertions.text: Identifies false sharing as the cause (fields share the same cache line)
Explains that writes to one field invalidate the cache line for other cores
Suggests cache-line padding (56-byte [56]byte array between fields) to separate cache lines
Mentions the 64-byte cache line size
Notes this should only be applied when profiling confirms contention

name: ilp-multi-accumulator
description: Tests knowledge of instruction-level parallelism with multiple accumulators
trap: The single accumulator creates a dependency chain — each addition waits for the previous one. The skill teaches using 4 independent accumulators to exploit CPU instruction-level parallelism (2-4x improvement). Without it, the model may suggest SIMD or goroutine-based parallelism rather than the simpler multi-accumulator approach.

assertions.text: Identifies the sequential dependency chain as the bottleneck (each addition waits for the previous)
Suggests using multiple accumulators (e.g., 4) for instruction-level parallelism
Shows code with 4 independent accumulators summing every 4th element
Handles the remainder elements (when len(data) is not divisible by 4)
Mentions expected 2-4x improvement from ILP

name: index-based-tree-cache-locality
description: Tests knowledge that index-based data structures beat pointer-based for cache locality
trap: The skill specifically teaches that index-based structures store nodes in a contiguous array with cache-friendly access, while pointer-based structures scatter nodes across the heap causing random cache misses. Without it, the model may prefer pointers for flexibility without discussing cache effects.

assertions.text: Recommends Option B (index-based) for better cache locality
Explains that pointer-based nodes are scattered across the heap causing cache misses
Explains that index-based nodes are stored in a contiguous array
Mentions CPU cache lines or memory prefetching as the reason for the difference

name: tight-loop-scheduler-starvation
description: Tests knowledge of tight CPU loops starving the Go scheduler
trap: A tight CPU loop with fully inlined operations may not yield to the scheduler, despite Go 1.14+ async preemption. The skill teaches using non-inlined function calls as preemption points, or //go:noinline. Without it, the model may suggest runtime.Gosched() (which works but isn't the recommended approach) or parallelism.

assertions.text: Explains that tight CPU loops with inlined operations can delay scheduler preemption
Suggests breaking the work into batches processed by a non-inlined function call
Mentions //go:noinline as an option to force preemption points
Explains the tradeoff: //go:noinline adds function call overhead but ensures scheduler fairness
Mentions that Go 1.14+ has async preemption but tight loops with inlined ops can still cause issues

name: reflect-deepequal-performance
description: Tests knowledge of reflect.DeepEqual being 50-200x slower than typed comparisons
trap: reflect.DeepEqual is 50-200x slower than typed comparison. The skill specifically calls this out as a common mistake and recommends slices.Equal, maps.Equal for the structured fields. Without it, the model may say it's fine or suggest a less specific alternative.

assertions.text: Identifies reflect.DeepEqual as 50-200x slower than typed comparison
Suggests using slices.Equal for the Hosts field
Suggests using maps.Equal for the Settings field
Provides a hand-written typed comparison function

name: type-switch-vs-repeated-assertions
description: Tests knowledge that type switches dispatch in one evaluation vs repeated if-assertions
trap: Repeated type assertions evaluate the interface type multiple times. A type switch dispatches in a single evaluation. The skill specifically teaches this pattern.

assertions.text: Suggests replacing repeated type assertions with a type switch
Explains that a type switch dispatches in a single evaluation
Shows the switch v := v.(type) { case string: ... case int: ... } pattern

name: http-transport-maxidleconnsperhost
description: Tests knowledge that default http.Client MaxIdleConnsPerHost is only 2
trap: The default http.Transport has MaxIdleConnsPerHost=2. With 200 concurrent goroutines, 198 connections are created and destroyed for each request. The skill specifically calls this out as a common mistake. Without it, the model may suggest connection pool libraries instead of tuning the built-in transport.

assertions.text: Identifies MaxIdleConnsPerHost defaulting to 2 as the root cause
Suggests configuring http.Transport with higher MaxIdleConnsPerHost (e.g., 20-100)
Shows complete Transport configuration with MaxIdleConns, MaxIdleConnsPerHost, and MaxConnsPerHost
Mentions draining resp.Body for connection reuse (io.Copy to io.Discard)
Does NOT suggest using a third-party connection pool library as the primary solution

name: response-body-drain
description: Tests knowledge that HTTP connections are only reused when the body is fully read
trap: Connections are only returned to the pool when the body is fully read. Even if you don't need the body, you must drain it with io.Copy(io.Discard, resp.Body). Without the skill, the model may focus on the Close() being present and miss the drain requirement.

assertions.text: Identifies that the response body is not being fully read/drained
Explains that connections are only reused when the body is fully consumed
Suggests adding io.Copy(io.Discard, resp.Body) before or after the status check

name: streaming-vs-readall
description: Tests knowledge of streaming vs buffering for large payloads
trap: io.ReadAll loads the entire response into memory. For a 5GB file, that's a 5GB allocation. The fix is io.Copy which streams with a 32KB buffer. The skill specifically warns about io.ReadAll for large payloads.

assertions.text: Identifies io.ReadAll as the cause of OOM (loads entire file into memory)
Suggests using io.Copy(w, resp.Body) to stream with constant memory
Mentions the 32KB internal buffer of io.Copy
Notes that io.ReadAll is fine for small, bounded payloads (< 1MB)

name: json-streaming-decoder
description: Tests knowledge of json.NewDecoder for streaming large JSON payloads
trap: json.Unmarshal buffers the entire body. json.NewDecoder streams tokens. The skill teaches the decoder.More() + decoder.Decode() pattern for processing one item at a time. Without it, the model may suggest chunking or pagination rather than streaming JSON.

assertions.text: Suggests using json.NewDecoder with r.Body directly (no io.ReadAll)
Shows the dec.More() + dec.Decode(&item) streaming pattern
Explains that this processes one item at a time with O(1) memory per item

name: cgo-overhead-tight-loop
description: Tests knowledge of cgo call overhead (~50-100ns per crossing) and batching strategy
trap: Each cgo call costs ~50-100ns due to stack switching. For math.Sqrt, the pure Go stdlib is equally fast and inlineable. For unavoidable C code, batch the call. The skill teaches both approaches. Without it, the model may not know the cgo overhead magnitude or suggest batching.

assertions.text: Identifies cgo overhead (~50-100ns per call) as the bottleneck in the tight loop
Suggests using math.Sqrt (pure Go, inlineable) instead of C.sqrt
For unavoidable C code, suggests batching: pass the entire array to C in one call
Mentions that goroutine is pinned to OS thread during cgo calls

name: gogc-gomemlimit-container
description: Tests knowledge of GOMEMLIMIT for containerized applications
prompt:
Our Go service runs in a Kubernetes pod with 512MB memory limit. It periodically gets OOM killed even though heap usage appears to be only 200MB when checked via runtime.MemStats.Alloc.

How should we configure the Go runtime for this container?
trap: The service needs GOMEMLIMIT set to ~80-90% of container memory (400-450MiB). Without it, the GC doesn't know about the container limit and may let the heap grow too large. The skill specifically calls this out as a common mistake ('No GC tuning in containers'). Without it, the model may suggest GOGC tuning alone.

assertions.text: Recommends setting GOMEMLIMIT to 80-90% of the container memory limit (400-450MiB)
Explains that the GC needs GOMEMLIMIT to know about the container's memory ceiling
Shows the GOMEMLIMIT=450MiB environment variable or debug.SetMemoryLimit equivalent
Explains the gap between Alloc and container limit (goroutine stacks, OS buffers, non-heap memory)
Does NOT recommend the ballast pattern (obsolete since Go 1.19)

name: ballast-pattern-obsolete
description: Tests that the model recommends GOMEMLIMIT over the ballast pattern
prompt:
I found this code in our Go service to reduce GC frequency. Is this still a recommended pattern?

```go
var ballast [1 << 30]byte // 1 GB ballast to reduce GC frequency

func main() {
    _ = ballast
    startServer()
}
```

We're running Go 1.22.
trap: The ballast pattern is obsolete since Go 1.19. GOMEMLIMIT is strictly better — it provides the same benefit without wasting physical memory. Without the skill, the model may say it's a valid approach or suggest minor improvements.

assertions.text: Identifies the ballast pattern as obsolete since Go 1.19
Recommends GOMEMLIMIT as the replacement
Explains that GOMEMLIMIT provides the same benefit without wasting physical memory
Shows the GOMEMLIMIT environment variable or debug.SetMemoryLimit call

name: gomaxprocs-container-go125
description: Tests knowledge of Go 1.25+ container-aware GOMAXPROCS vs automaxprocs
prompt:
Our Go service runs in a container with 2 CPU cores on a 64-core host. We're on Go 1.25. A colleague suggested adding `go.uber.org/automaxprocs`. Is that necessary?

```go
import _ "go.uber.org/automaxprocs"

func main() {
    startServer()
}
```
trap: Go 1.25+ automatically detects container CPU limits (cgroup v1/v2). automaxprocs is unnecessary. For Go 1.24 and earlier, it IS needed. The skill makes this version-dependent distinction clear.

assertions.text: States that Go 1.25+ automatically detects container CPU limits
Recommends removing the automaxprocs dependency
Mentions that automaxprocs IS needed for Go 1.24 and earlier
Mentions cgroup CPU quota detection as the mechanism

name: pgo-workflow
description: Tests knowledge of Profile-Guided Optimization workflow and expected gains
prompt: We want to improve our Go service's performance with minimal code changes. The service is interface-heavy with many small methods. We're on Go 1.22. What low-effort optimization can we apply?
trap: PGO (Profile-Guided Optimization) gives 2-7% improvement with minimal effort: collect production profile, save as default.pgo, rebuild. The skill specifically describes when PGO helps most (interface calls, hot inlining). Without it, the model may suggest code-level optimizations rather than the build-level PGO approach.

assertions.text: Recommends Profile-Guided Optimization (PGO)
Describes the workflow: collect production CPU profile, save as default.pgo, rebuild
Mentions expected improvement of 2-7%
Explains PGO benefits: more aggressive inlining and devirtualization of interface calls
Notes that profiles should be refreshed after significant code changes

name: slog-logattrs-hot-path
description: Tests knowledge of slog.LogAttrs for zero-allocation logging when level is disabled
trap: Even with slog, arguments are evaluated before the level check. The 'data' field is boxed into any, allocating. The skill teaches slog.LogAttrs with typed attributes (slog.Int, slog.String) for zero allocations when the level is disabled. Without it, the model may suggest level checks or not know about LogAttrs.

assertions.text: Explains that log arguments are evaluated/boxed before the level check
Recommends slog.LogAttrs for zero allocations when level is disabled
Shows typed attributes: slog.Int("id", item.ID), slog.String("name", item.Name)
Notes that slog.Any can still allocate even with slog, so typed attributes are preferred

name: regexp-compile-per-call
description: Tests knowledge of compiled pattern caching vs per-call compilation
trap: regexp.Compile/MustCompile parses the pattern into a state machine (~5,700ns) on every call. The match itself is ~450ns. The skill quantifies the 10-12x waste. Fix: compile at package level. Without the skill, the model may suggest simpler regex or string operations instead of caching.

assertions.text: Identifies that regexp compilation happens on every call (~5,700ns per compile)
Suggests moving regexp.MustCompile to package-level variables
Notes that compiled regexps are safe for concurrent use
Quantifies the waste (10-12x overhead from recompilation vs match-only)

name: singleflight-cache-stampede
description: Tests knowledge of singleflight for cache stampede prevention
trap: This is a cache stampede problem. The skill teaches singleflight.Group to deduplicate concurrent requests for the same key. Without it, the model may suggest mutex locking (which blocks all cities) or probabilistic TTL (which doesn't fully prevent the stampede).

assertions.text: Identifies this as a cache stampede problem
Recommends golang.org/x/sync/singleflight.Group
Shows sf.Do(key, func) pattern where only one goroutine fetches while others wait
Does NOT suggest a global mutex as the primary solution (it would block all cities)

name: algorithmic-complexity-slice-contains-loop
description: Tests knowledge of algorithmic complexity traps (O(n*m) from slices.Contains in a loop)
trap: slices.Contains in a loop creates O(n*m) complexity. The skill teaches building a map[T]struct{} first for O(n+m). Without it, the model may suggest sorting + binary search (O(n log n)) rather than the optimal map approach.

assertions.text: Identifies O(n*m) complexity from slices.Contains inside a loop
Suggests building a map[string]struct{} from the valid slice first
Shows the O(n+m) solution with map lookup
Uses struct{} (0 bytes) for the map value type, not bool

name: early-return-full-scan
description: Tests knowledge of early returns to avoid full collection scans
trap: The function always iterates the full collection even after finding a match. The skill teaches early return to avoid unnecessary iterations. This is a subtle inefficiency that many miss.

assertions.text: Identifies that the function always scans the full collection even after finding a match
Adds an early return (return true) when the first expired session is found
Removes the found variable in favor of direct returns

name: iterator-chain-vs-direct-loop
description: Tests knowledge that iterator chains create closure overhead vs direct loops
trap: The skill specifically warns against iterator chains in hot paths: they create closures and intermediate machinery. The direct loop is simpler, faster, and supports early return. Without the skill, the model may say both are equivalent or prefer the iterator for readability.

assertions.text: Recommends Option B (direct loop) for performance
Explains that iterator chains create closures and intermediate allocations
Notes that Filter processes ALL elements before First can pick one, while the loop short-circuits
Acknowledges that iterator chains are fine for non-hot paths where readability matters more

name: indirect-function-calls-closure
description: Tests knowledge that closure indirection prevents inlining in generic wrappers
trap: The closure passed to Map prevents inlining at the call site. The skill teaches replacing indirect function calls (Map + closure) with direct loops for 13-17% improvement. Without it, the model may not know that the closure indirection is the bottleneck.

assertions.text: Suggests replacing the Map+closure pattern with a direct loop
Explains that the closure/function call indirection prevents inlining
Shows the direct loop: result[i] = *ptrs[i]
Mentions the expected improvement range (13-17% or similar)

name: http-server-no-timeouts
description: Tests knowledge that zero-value http.Server has NO timeouts
prompt:
Is there a performance or security issue with this Go HTTP server setup?

```go
func main() {
    http.HandleFunc("/api/data", handleData)
    http.ListenAndServe(":8080", nil)
}
```
trap: The zero-value http.Server has no timeouts. A slow or malicious client can hold connections indefinitely (Slowloris attack), exhausting file descriptors and memory. The skill lists ReadTimeout, WriteTimeout, and IdleTimeout as required. Without it, the model may focus on other issues.

assertions.text: Identifies that the default http.Server has NO timeouts
Mentions Slowloris attack or slow client holding connections indefinitely
Suggests setting ReadTimeout, WriteTimeout, and IdleTimeout
Shows creating an explicit http.Server struct with timeout values

name: http-keepalive-crawler
description: Tests knowledge of disabling keep-alive for crawlers hitting many hosts
trap: For crawlers hitting many different hosts, idle connections accumulate because MaxIdleConns caps total idle connections but each host has up to 10 idle. With 100K hosts, connections pile up. The skill teaches DisableKeepAlives: true for this use case. Without it, the model may suggest lowering MaxIdleConnsPerHost instead of disabling keep-alive entirely.

assertions.text: Identifies that idle connections accumulate across many different hosts
Suggests DisableKeepAlives: true for the crawler client
Explains that keep-alive is counterproductive when crawling many unique hosts

name: buffered-io-syscall-reduction
description: Tests knowledge of bufio for reducing syscall count
trap: Unbuffered file writes issue a syscall per WriteString call. The skill teaches bufio.Writer which batches writes, reducing syscalls by 10x. Without it, the model may suggest strings.Builder or bytes.Buffer instead of buffered I/O.

assertions.text: Identifies that each WriteString issues a separate syscall
Suggests using bufio.NewWriter(f) to batch writes
Includes w.Flush() at the end
Does NOT primarily suggest strings.Builder (which doesn't write to a file)

name: concurrent-pipeline-when-not-to-use
description: Tests knowledge of when concurrent pipelines are NOT beneficial
prompt:
Our Go data pipeline has 3 stages. We want to make it faster by running stages concurrently:

1. Stage A: Compress data (CPU-bound)
2. Stage B: Encrypt data (CPU-bound)
3. Stage C: Calculate checksum (CPU-bound)

All stages are CPU-bound. Should we run them concurrently in goroutines with channels between stages?
trap: When all stages compete for the same resource (CPU), concurrency adds context-switching overhead with no resource utilization gain. The skill explicitly says 'If A and B both compete for CPU, concurrency causes context-switching overhead with no resource utilization gain.' Without it, the model may recommend the concurrent pipeline pattern.

assertions.text: Recommends AGAINST concurrent pipelines for this case
Explains that all three stages compete for the same resource (CPU)
Notes that concurrency only helps when stages saturate DIFFERENT resources
Mentions context-switching overhead as a cost of unnecessary concurrency
Suggests sequential processing or batching as a simpler alternative

name: batch-db-inserts
description: Tests knowledge of batch database inserts vs row-by-row
trap: 50K individual inserts means 50K round-trips. The skill teaches batching with multi-row INSERT or COPY protocol. Without it, the model may suggest connection pooling or prepared statements (which help but don't address the fundamental round-trip overhead).

assertions.text: Identifies individual inserts as the problem (50K round-trips)
Suggests batch inserts (multi-row VALUES or COPY protocol)
Shows a batching pattern with configurable batch size
Wraps batches in transactions for atomicity
Does NOT suggest only connection pooling or prepared statements as the primary fix

name: panic-recover-control-flow
description: Tests knowledge that panic/recover should not be used for control flow
trap: strconv.Atoi returns an error, not a panic. The defer/recover is unnecessary overhead: panic allocates a stack trace and unwinds the stack. The skill specifically warns against panic/recover as control flow. Without it, the model may accept the pattern as defensive programming.

assertions.text: Identifies that panic/recover is unnecessary since strconv.Atoi returns errors
Explains that panic allocates a stack trace and unwinds the stack (10-100x overhead)
Suggests using simple error checking: v, err := strconv.Atoi(s)
States that panic/recover should only be used for truly unrecoverable situations

name: monotonic-time-since
description: Tests knowledge of time.Since using monotonic clock for accurate elapsed time
trap: time.Since(start) uses the monotonic clock component, which is immune to wall-clock adjustments (NTP, DST). The skill specifically teaches this pattern. The code shown actually works correctly because time.Now() captures monotonic time, but time.Since is slightly more efficient. The key insight is ensuring the start time captures monotonic time.

assertions.text: Suggests using time.Since(start) for elapsed time measurement
Explains that time.Since uses the monotonic clock, immune to NTP/wall-clock adjustments
Notes that time.Now() already captures monotonic time for Sub() operations

name: prometheus-gc-pressure-queries
description: Tests knowledge of specific PromQL queries for GC pressure monitoring
prompt: Our Go service in production has occasional latency spikes. We suspect GC pauses. We have Prometheus monitoring with default Go metrics. What PromQL queries should we use to diagnose GC pressure?
trap: The skill provides specific PromQL queries for GC diagnosis. Without it, the model may suggest generic approaches or incorrect metric names. The key queries are rate(go_gc_duration_seconds_count[5m]) for frequency and the worst-case pause quantile.

assertions.text: Provides rate(go_gc_duration_seconds_count[5m]) for GC frequency
Provides go_gc_duration_seconds{quantile="1"} for worst-case GC pause
Mentions >2 cycles/s sustained as a signal of excessive allocation rate
Suggests rate(go_memstats_alloc_bytes_total[5m]) for allocation rate monitoring

name: goroutine-leak-prometheus
description: Tests knowledge of PromQL for detecting goroutine leaks
prompt: We suspect our Go service has a goroutine leak in production. The service gets slower over time and eventually needs to be restarted. What Prometheus queries can confirm a goroutine leak?
trap: The skill provides specific goroutine leak PromQL queries. go_goroutines should correlate with load; growing independently of traffic indicates a leak. delta(go_goroutines[1h]) shows net change.

assertions.text: Provides go_goroutines metric for goroutine count monitoring
Suggests delta(go_goroutines[1h]) for detecting net goroutine increase over time
Notes that goroutine count should correlate with load — growing independently means leak

name: continuous-profiling-tools
description: Tests knowledge of continuous profiling tools and their tradeoffs
prompt: We want to detect performance regressions across deployments in production. Our Go service runs on Kubernetes. We need historical profiling data to compare flamegraphs between versions. What tools should we evaluate?
trap: The skill lists specific continuous profiling tools with overhead and best-for guidance: Grafana Pyroscope (push/pull, 2-5%), Parca (eBPF, <1%), Datadog, GCP Profiler. Without it, the model may only suggest pprof endpoints without mentioning continuous profiling platforms.

assertions.text: Recommends Grafana Pyroscope, Parca, or similar continuous profiling platform
Mentions overhead estimates (1-5% range)
Describes push vs pull collection modes
Mentions historical flamegraph comparison as a key feature
Suggests feeding profiles into PGO for build optimization

name: gogc-high-vs-low-tradeoff
description: Tests knowledge of GOGC tuning tradeoffs (latency vs throughput)
prompt:
We have two Go services:
- Service A: Latency-sensitive API (p99 target: 10ms)
- Service B: Batch data processor (throughput target: 1M records/min)

Both have default GOGC=100. Should we tune GOGC differently for each? What values would you recommend?
trap: The skill teaches that GOGC=50 trades memory for lower latency (more frequent, shorter pauses) while GOGC=200 reduces GC frequency for throughput. Without it, the model may suggest the same value for both or not know the tradeoff.

assertions.text: Recommends lower GOGC (e.g., 50) for Service A (latency-sensitive)
Recommends higher GOGC (e.g., 200) for Service B (throughput-oriented)
Explains the tradeoff: lower GOGC = more frequent but shorter pauses
Explains the tradeoff: higher GOGC = less frequent GC but more memory used

name: godebug-gctrace
description: Tests knowledge of GODEBUG=gctrace=1 output interpretation
prompt:
We ran our Go service with GODEBUG=gctrace=1 and got this output:

```
gc 142 @23.456s 12%: 0.015+89+1.2 ms clock, 0.3+72/150+24 ms cpu, 180->340->200 MB, 400 MB goal, 8 P
```

Interpret this GC trace line. What does it tell us about the service's health?
trap: The skill provides a field-by-field breakdown of gctrace output. The 12% CPU, 89ms pause, and 180->340->200 MB heap growth are concerning. Without the skill, the model may not correctly parse all fields or identify the implications.

assertions.text: Correctly identifies gc 142 as the 142nd GC cycle
Identifies 12% as total CPU time spent in GC (which is high)
Interprets 180->340->200 MB as heap before, peak during, and after collection
Identifies 400 MB goal as the target heap size based on GOGC/GOMEMLIMIT
Notes that 12% GC CPU is concerning and suggests reducing allocation rate or tuning GOGC

name: unsafe-without-benchmark-proof
description: Tests that the model warns against premature unsafe usage
trap: The skill states unsafe is 'Only justified when profiling shows >10% improvement in a verified hot path.' At 100 req/s, this is not a hot path and the copy cost is negligible. Without the skill, the model may accept the optimization or only warn about safety without the benchmark threshold.

assertions.text: Recommends against using unsafe here
Notes that 100 req/s is not a hot path where this optimization is justified
States that unsafe requires benchmark proof showing >10% improvement
Mentions safety risks of unsafe (mutating string backing store, GC interaction)

name: precomputed-lookup-table
description: Tests knowledge of precomputed lookup tables for pure functions with small input space
trap: The function is pure with a 16-element input space per nibble. The skill teaches precomputed lookup tables for this exact pattern. Two array lookups are faster than conditional branches. Without the skill, the model may suggest bitwise tricks rather than the lookup table approach.

assertions.text: Suggests a precomputed lookup table (e.g., var hexDigit = [16]byte{...})
Shows the table lookup: hexDigit[b>>4], hexDigit[b&0x0f]
Explains that the lookup table fits in L1 cache and is faster than branching

name: json-performance-alternatives
description: Tests knowledge of JSON performance alternatives beyond encoding/json
prompt:
Our Go API server spends 40% of CPU time in encoding/json according to pprof. We serialize thousands of Response structs per second. What options do we have to speed up JSON encoding?

```go
type Response struct {
    ID        int       `json:"id"`
    Name      string    `json:"name"`
    Values    []float64 `json:"values"`
    Metadata  map[string]string `json:"metadata"`
    CreatedAt time.Time `json:"created_at"`
}
```
trap: The skill lists specific alternatives: custom MarshalJSON methods, code-gen libraries (easyjson, ffjson), drop-in replacements (goccy/go-json, json-iterator, bytedance/sonic), and experimental encoding/json/v2. Without it, the model may only suggest one approach.

assertions.text: Mentions custom MarshalJSON/UnmarshalJSON methods as an option
Mentions code-generation libraries (easyjson, ffjson)
Mentions drop-in replacement libraries (goccy/go-json, json-iterator, or bytedance/sonic)
Explains that encoding/json uses reflection which causes CPU and allocation overhead
Quantifies expected improvement (2-5x or similar)

name: channel-batch-processing
description: Tests knowledge of batch processing from channels with timeout flush
prompt:
Our Go service receives events from a channel and needs to batch them for bulk database insert. Events arrive at varying rates. We need to flush either when the batch is full (1000 items) OR after a timeout (100ms), whichever comes first.

Design the batch processor function.
trap: The skill shows the exact pattern: select on channel + ticker, with batch accumulation, flush on size threshold or timer. The key detail is reusing batch via batch[:0] and handling the channel close. Without the skill, the model may miss the ticker-based timeout or the clean shutdown.

assertions.text: Uses select with both channel receive and ticker/timer for timeout
Flushes on batch size threshold
Flushes on timeout (ticker)
Handles channel close (flushes remaining items)
Reuses the batch slice (batch[:0] or similar) to reduce allocations

name: allocation-reduction-vs-gc-tuning
description: Tests knowledge that reducing allocations is better than tuning GOGC
prompt: Our Go service has high GC overhead (8% CPU). Should we increase GOGC to reduce GC frequency, or is there a better approach?
trap: The skill explicitly states: 'Reducing allocations helps more than tuning GOGC — it addresses the root cause instead of managing the symptom.' Without it, the model may recommend GOGC tuning as the primary solution.

assertions.text: Recommends reducing allocations as the primary approach over GOGC tuning
Explains that GOGC tuning manages the symptom while allocation reduction addresses the root cause
Suggests specific allocation reduction strategies (value types, sync.Pool, preallocation, avoid interface boxing)
Acknowledges GOGC tuning as a secondary measure after allocation reduction

name: gctrace-key-fields
description: Tests knowledge of what to monitor in GC traces (frequency, pause times, CPU%)
prompt: We're monitoring our Go service with GODEBUG=gctrace=1. What specific patterns in the output should alarm us?
trap: The skill lists three key signals: GC frequency (too often = too many allocations), pause times (high = large heap or many pointers), CPU% (high = tune GOGC or reduce allocations). Without it, the model may give generic advice.

assertions.text: Mentions high GC frequency as a signal of too many allocations
Mentions high pause times as a signal of large heap or many pointers
Mentions high GC CPU% (>5%) as concerning
Provides the GODEBUG=gctrace=1 command or assumes it's already running

name: non-go-memory-leak-detection
description: Tests knowledge of detecting non-Go memory leaks via Prometheus
prompt: Our Go service uses cgo to call a C image processing library. process_resident_memory_bytes keeps growing but go_memstats_alloc_bytes is stable. What PromQL query helps diagnose this?
trap: The skill provides the specific PromQL: process_resident_memory_bytes - go_memstats_sys_bytes. A growing gap indicates non-Go memory (cgo, mmap). Without it, the model may suggest Go heap tools that won't find the C memory leak.

assertions.text: Suggests process_resident_memory_bytes - go_memstats_sys_bytes to isolate non-Go memory
Identifies this as a likely C/cgo memory leak (not a Go leak)
Explains that growing gap between RSS and Go sys bytes indicates non-Go memory growth
Suggests C-level memory profiling tools (valgrind, AddressSanitizer) for further diagnosis

name: cpu-saturation-prometheus
description: Tests knowledge of detecting CPU saturation via Prometheus
prompt: How do we detect if our Go service is CPU-saturated in production using Prometheus metrics?
trap: The skill provides specific PromQL: rate(process_cpu_seconds_total[5m]) / GOMAXPROCS. A ratio >0.8 sustained means CPU-saturated. Without the skill, the model may suggest system-level metrics rather than Go-specific ones.

assertions.text: Provides rate(process_cpu_seconds_total[5m]) for CPU cores consumed
Divides by GOMAXPROCS to get utilization ratio
States that >0.8 sustained indicates CPU saturation

name: document-optimizations
description: Tests whether the model recommends documenting optimizations with comments
prompt: I optimized a Go function from using reflect.DeepEqual to a hand-written comparison, and from column-first to row-first matrix traversal. Should I add comments explaining why?
trap: The skill's core philosophy #3 states: 'Document optimizations — add code comments explaining why a pattern is faster, with benchmark numbers when available. Future readers need context to avoid reverting an unnecessary optimization.' Without it, the model may skip this guidance.

assertions.text: Strongly recommends adding comments explaining WHY the optimization was made
Suggests including benchmark numbers in the comments
Explains that future readers may revert optimizations they don't understand

name: lru-cache-freelru
description: Tests knowledge of high-performance LRU cache alternatives
prompt: We need a bounded LRU cache in Go for our hot path. We considered using container/list from the standard library. Is that the best option for performance?
trap: The skill mentions that container/list has poor cache locality (each node is a separate heap allocation). It recommends elastic/go-freelru (37x faster, contiguous memory) or hashicorp/golang-lru. Without it, the model may recommend container/list as sufficient.

assertions.text: Notes that container/list has poor cache locality (separate heap allocation per node)
Recommends elastic/go-freelru or hashicorp/golang-lru as alternatives
Mentions the performance advantage of contiguous memory layouts for LRU

name: simd-when-not-worth
description: Tests knowledge of when SIMD is NOT worth pursuing
prompt: Our Go service's bottleneck is heap allocations in JSON parsing (60% of CPU time is in GC according to pprof). A colleague suggested using SIMD instructions to speed up the JSON parser. Should we pursue this?
trap: The skill explicitly states: 'If your bottleneck is allocations or I/O, SIMD won't help.' SIMD only helps CPU-bound numeric work. The bottleneck here is allocations/GC, not computation. Without the skill, the model may evaluate SIMD as a viable approach.

assertions.text: Recommends against SIMD for this case
Explains that the bottleneck is allocations/GC, not CPU-bound computation
States that SIMD only helps CPU-bound numeric inner loops
Suggests reducing allocations as the correct optimization approach

name: set-map-struct-zero-size
description: Tests knowledge of using struct{} vs bool for set maps
trap: The skill specifically calls out: 'Use struct{} (0 bytes) instead of bool (1 byte) for set maps.' While the difference is small per entry, it adds up with millions of entries. Without the skill, the model may say both are equivalent.

assertions.text: Recommends struct{} for set maps
Explains that struct{} is 0 bytes vs bool at 1 byte per entry

name: regression-detection-prometheus
description: Tests knowledge of PromQL queries for deployment regression detection
prompt: We just deployed a new version of our Go service. How can we use Prometheus to detect if this deployment introduced a performance regression?
trap: The skill provides specific regression detection PromQL: rate(go_memstats_alloc_bytes_total[5m]) for allocation rate comparison and histogram_quantile(0.99, ...) for p99 latency. Without it, the model may suggest generic monitoring.

assertions.text: Suggests comparing rate(go_memstats_alloc_bytes_total[5m]) before and after deploy
Suggests monitoring p99 latency histogram_quantile for increase after deploy
Mentions comparing metrics between old and new deployment versions

name: statsviz-development-profiling
description: Tests knowledge of real-time development visualization tools
prompt: I'm developing a Go service locally and want to see real-time GC behavior, heap usage, and goroutine count in a browser dashboard without setting up Prometheus/Grafana. What tool can I use?
trap: The skill specifically mentions statsviz (github.com/arl/statsviz) for real-time browser dashboard during local development. Without it, the model may suggest full monitoring stacks or pprof web UI which doesn't provide real-time visualization.

assertions.text: Recommends statsviz (github.com/arl/statsviz) for real-time browser visualization
Mentions the /debug/statsviz endpoint or statsviz.Register pattern
Notes that it shows heap, GC pauses, goroutines, and scheduler in real-time
